metric_name: IT spending share of US economic growth
notes: Kastner claims IT spending growth represents 25% of all U.S. economic growth, framing the macro opportunity.
Microsoft NT Server identified as actively gaining market share; mission-critical workloads becoming the norm on NT.
Kastner asserts that high availability has become affordable — a key market-readiness signal for Marathon's product.
Stratus labeled 'Stratus Radio' — implied jab at its proprietary, high-cost, narrow-market positioning relative to NT ecosystem.
Tandem/Compaq flagged as a named competitor but implicitly weakened by the 1997 Compaq acquisition and platform mismatch.

metric_name: Competitive strength of Microsoft Cluster Server
notes: Microsoft Cluster Server (Wolfpack) listed as competition but grouped under 'The Competition is Weak'; implies insufficient HA coverage.
Kastner states Intel/NT are not known for high availability — framing the credibility problem Marathon must overcome in sales.
Real competition identified as inertia: 'Hey everybody knows NT Server fails. We'll live with it.' and 'Nah it can't possibly work.' objections.
Recommended selling solution: use Fear Uncertainty and Doubt (FUD) — downtime cost stories, 99.9% availability arithmetic, 'crash could cost you your job'.
Specific tactic: quantify downtime cost using availability percentage math to create urgency with buyers.

metric_value: buy Microsofties a latte and demo
notes: First channel recommendation: engage Microsoft field reps with informal demos — leverage Microsoft's installed base and co-selling motion.

metric_value: target Big Six and regional SIs
notes: Kastner recommends power lunches with Big Six SIs, regional SIs, and ERP national support SIs as key channel partners.
Industrial equipment manufacturers cited as a channel opportunity alongside SIs.
Retail identified as a priority vertical for Marathon Endurance: in-store servers and logistics applications.
Healthcare named as a target vertical given mission-critical requirements for patient data and systems uptime.

metric_name: Target vertical — public safety / command and control
metric_value: public safety and command and control
notes: Public safety and command-and-control identified as verticals requiring continuous availability — natural fit for Endurance.
Utilities deregulation wave cited as creating new HA requirements; a timely vertical opportunity in 1998.
Financial services named as a target vertical given zero-tolerance for downtime in transaction processing.
Transportation listed among vertical market targets for high-availability NT solutions.
Local ISPs cited as a target market; Yahoo named as an example prospect — reflecting 1998 e-commerce and internet infrastructure boom.
BackOffice VARs identified as a natural channel partner: already selling NT-based solutions to business customers.

metric_value: local general-business speeches and country-club schmoozing
notes: Kastner recommends face-to-face community engagement (local speeches, country club) reflecting the relationship-driven B2B sales culture of the era.

metric_value: ERP evolving into Supply Chain Management
notes: Kastner identifies Enterprise Resource Planning transitioning to Supply Chain Management as a driver of new mission-critical NT workloads.
Electronic commerce listed as a key emerging workload; prescient given the e-commerce explosion of 1999-2001.

metric_value: time is right for Marathon Endurance
notes: Overall thesis: the convergence of IT spending growth, NT adoption, and mission-critical requirements makes 1998 the right moment for Marathon to aggressively sell Endurance.
